research_series.series_name: Sleep, Circadian State, Autonomic-Metabolic-Inflammatory Changes, Fatigue, Mood, and Cognition

patterns.pattern_name: Insufficient or disrupted sleep reduces HRV and shifts autonomic balance toward sympathetic predominance

mediator.name: reduced_heart_rate_variability
mediator.pathway: sleep loss and fragmentation reduce parasympathetic/vagal modulation and increase sympathetic predominance
mediator.mechanism: reduced RMSSD/HF-HRV, increased LF/LF-HF indices, elevated resting heart rate and blood pressure

inference_rule.plain_language_rule: Insufficient, fragmented, or disrupted sleep increases the likelihood of autonomic imbalance, especially reduced HRV and sympathetic predominance, which is associated with next-day fatigue, mood worsening, and impaired cognitive performance.

hypotheses.reason: Multiple meta-analyses and experimental studies link sleep deprivation/restriction/fragmentation to reduced HRV, elevated resting heart rate/blood pressure, and adverse neurobehavioral outcomes.

evidence.summary: The evidence supports a robust relationship between sleep deprivation/restriction/fragmentation and autonomic dysregulation, especially reduced HRV and increased sympathetic activity.

key_sources.title: Effects of sleep deprivation on heart rate variability: a systematic review and meta-analysis
key_sources.relevant_finding: Sleep deprivation reduces HRV and indicates autonomic imbalance.
key_sources.limitations: HRV protocols and sleep deprivation designs vary.

key_sources.title: Sleep Deprivation Deteriorates Heart Rate Variability and Photoplethysmography
key_sources.relevant_finding: Sleep deprivation deteriorates HRV and peripheral cardiovascular signals.
key_sources.limitations: Acute sleep deprivation may not generalize to chronic insomnia.

key_sources.title: Effects of sleep fragmentation and partial sleep restriction on heart rate variability during night
key_sources.relevant_finding: Sleep fragmentation and partial restriction alter nocturnal HRV.
key_sources.limitations: Nighttime HRV effects may differ from daytime wearable signals.

limitations: HRV is sensitive to measurement protocol and time of day.
Insomnia symptoms alone show mixed HRV findings unless paired with objective short sleep or comorbidities.
Full sleep-to-HRV-to-cognition mediation is not consistently tested.
alternative_explanations: Stress may cause both poor sleep and reduced HRV.
Sleep apnea or cardiometabolic disease may confound associations.
Caffeine and medications can alter HRV independently.

patterns.pattern_name: Circadian misalignment impairs glucose regulation and increases cardiometabolic/inflammatory risk independent of total sleep time

mediator.name: metabolic_and_inflammatory_dysregulation
mediator.pathway: circadian timing mismatch disrupts metabolic, endocrine, and inflammatory regulation
mediator.mechanism: reduced insulin sensitivity, increased glycemic variability, elevated CRP/IL-6/TNF-alpha/resistin, increased blood pressure

inference_rule.plain_language_rule: Circadian misalignment can worsen metabolic and inflammatory state even when total sleep duration is controlled, increasing risk for fatigue, mood disturbance, and impaired cognition.

hypotheses.reason: Human laboratory and shift-work studies show circadian misalignment independently worsens glucose regulation, inflammatory markers, cardiovascular markers, and cognitive/mood outcomes.

evidence.summary: Circadian misalignment has independent adverse effects on glucose regulation, insulin sensitivity, inflammatory markers, blood pressure, and neurobehavioral function.

key_sources.title: Adverse metabolic and cardiovascular consequences of circadian misalignment
key_sources.relevant_finding: Circadian misalignment worsens metabolic and cardiovascular outcomes independent of sleep duration.
key_sources.limitations: Laboratory protocol may not capture all real-world shift-work complexity.

key_sources.title: Circadian Misalignment Augments Markers of Insulin Resistance and Inflammation, Independently of Sleep Loss
key_sources.relevant_finding: Circadian misalignment increases insulin resistance and inflammation beyond sleep loss alone.

key_sources.title: Adverse Metabolic Consequences in Humans of Prolonged Sleep Restriction Combined with Circadian Disruption
key_sources.relevant_finding: Combined sleep restriction and circadian disruption produce adverse metabolic consequences.
key_sources.limitations: Intensive lab design; ecological validity should be interpreted cautiously.

limitations: Circadian misalignment overlaps with sleep restriction, light exposure, meal timing, and work stress in real-world settings.
Mediation from metabolic/inflammatory markers to cognition is not always directly tested.
alternative_explanations: Night shift work may include stress, altered diet, and reduced physical activity.
Chronotype mismatch may reflect behavioral rather than biological effects.

patterns.pattern_name: Chronic sleep restriction produces cumulative cognitive impairment even when subjective sleepiness or fatigue plateaus

mediator.name: sleep_pressure_and_neurobehavioral_instability
mediator.pathway: cumulative wakefulness and insufficient recovery sleep degrade sustained attention and executive control
mediator.mechanism: progressive lapses in vigilance and cognitive throughput, with subjective adaptation/underestimation

inference_rule.plain_language_rule: Repeated nights of insufficient sleep can cause cumulative impairments in attention, executive function, and working memory, even when the person no longer subjectively feels progressively worse.

hypotheses.reason: Dose-response experimental sleep restriction data show cumulative neurobehavioral deficits, while subjective fatigue/sleepiness may plateau.

evidence.summary: Experimental dose-response evidence shows chronic sleep restriction produces cumulative cognitive deficits equivalent to acute total sleep deprivation, while subjective sleepiness may underestimate impairment.

key_sources.title: The cumulative cost of additional wakefulness: dose-response effects on neurobehavioral functions and sleep physiology from chronic sleep restriction and total sleep deprivation
key_sources.relevant_finding: Chronic restriction to six hours or less per night produces cumulative deficits in attention/executive function/working memory comparable to total sleep deprivation.
key_sources.limitations: Laboratory restriction may not capture compensatory strategies in real life.

limitations: Subjective fatigue is not a reliable proxy for objective cognitive impairment.
alternative_explanations: Motivation and task learning can mask some deficits.
Circadian phase and caffeine intake may alter observed performance.

patterns.pattern_name: Sleep disturbance is associated with elevated inflammatory markers, especially CRP and IL-6, but effects are modest and variable

mediator.name: inflammatory_markers
mediator.pathway: sleep disturbance activates inflammatory signaling and interacts with circadian disruption
mediator.mechanism: elevated CRP and IL-6, variable TNF-alpha effects, stronger inflammatory response when sleep restriction combines with circadian misalignment

inference_rule.plain_language_rule: Poor or disturbed sleep increases the likelihood of modest inflammatory activation, especially CRP and IL-6, which may contribute to fatigue and mood/cognitive symptoms, but this full mediation chain is not consistently proven.

hypotheses.reason: Meta-analytic evidence links sleep disturbance to CRP/IL-6, but effect sizes are modest and mediation to neurobehavioral symptoms is often indirect.

evidence.summary: Poor sleep/disturbed sleep is associated with increased CRP and IL-6. TNF-alpha findings are less consistent, and full mediator chains to fatigue/cognition require caution.

key_sources.title: Sleep Disturbance, Sleep Duration, and Inflammation: A Systematic Review and Meta-Analysis of Cohort Studies and Experimental Sleep Deprivation
key_sources.relevant_finding: Sleep disturbance and duration are associated with inflammatory markers, especially CRP and IL-6.
key_sources.population: human cohorts and experimental sleep deprivation studies
key_sources.limitations: Effect sizes modest; heterogeneity across studies.

key_sources.title: Circadian Misalignment Augments Markers of Insulin Resistance and Inflammation, Independently of Sleep Loss
key_sources.relevant_finding: Circadian misalignment augments inflammation and insulin resistance independent of sleep loss.

limitations: Inflammatory effects are modest and heterogeneous.
TNF-alpha findings are not uniformly consistent.
Fatigue/cognition mediation is usually not directly tested.
alternative_explanations: Adiposity, infection, depression, and stress may drive both poor sleep and inflammation.
Sleep apnea may explain inflammatory findings in some cohorts.

negative_or_uncertain_findings.claim: Insomnia symptoms alone do not reliably predict HRV impairment without objective short sleep duration or comorbidity.
negative_or_uncertain_findings.reason: The report summarizes a critical review finding high bias and conflicting results unless insomnia co-occurs with objective short total sleep time or conditions such as OSA.
CRP and IL-6 evidence is stronger; TNF-alpha effects are reported as modest, variable, or sometimes null.

negative_or_uncertain_findings.claim: Subjective fatigue reliably tracks objective cognitive impairment during chronic sleep restriction.
negative_or_uncertain_findings.reason: Dose-response sleep restriction data show subjective fatigue/sleepiness can plateau while objective attention/executive deficits continue accumulating.

high_value_candidate_nodes.definition: Sleep duration below individual or population-recommended needs, often operationalized as less than six hours per night in experimental or cohort studies.
Repeated disruption of sleep continuity, reducing restorative sleep even when time in bed is preserved.
Mismatch between endogenous circadian phase and behavioral sleep/wake, eating, or work schedule.
Lower variability in beat-to-beat heart intervals, often reflecting reduced parasympathetic/vagal modulation or autonomic imbalance.
Higher resting heart rate, often interpreted with context as sympathetic activation or reduced recovery.
Reduced tissue response to insulin, impairing glucose regulation.
Higher circulating interleukin-6, an inflammatory cytokine linked to sleep disturbance and sickness-behavior-like symptoms.
Higher C-reactive protein, a systemic inflammation marker associated with poor sleep and circadian disruption.
Subjective fatigue or low energy on the day following insufficient or disrupted sleep.
Reduced sustained attention or increased vigilance lapses following sleep loss or circadian disruption.

source_verification_queue.title: Effects of sleep deprivation on heart rate variability: a systematic review and meta-analysis
source_verification_queue.reason: Recent source; verify DOI, publication details, inclusion criteria, and HRV effect sizes.

source_verification_queue.title: Sleep loss and emotion: A systematic review and meta-analysis of over 50 years of experimental research
source_verification_queue.reason: Important for mood output layer; extract domain-specific emotion results.

source_verification_queue.title: The cumulative cost of additional wakefulness
source_verification_queue.reason: Core source for cumulative cognitive impairment; verify exact sleep restriction groups and outcomes.

source_verification_queue.title: Circadian Misalignment Augments Markers of Insulin Resistance and Inflammation, Independently of Sleep Loss
source_verification_queue.reason: Core source for independent circadian effect; verify biomarkers and protocol.

source_verification_queue.title: Heart rate variability in insomnia patients: A critical review of the literature
source_verification_queue.reason: Needed to avoid overgeneralizing insomnia symptoms to HRV impairment.

research_gaps: Need within-person mediation models linking specific sleep exposure types to physiological mediators and next-day neurobehavioral outcomes.
Need better separation of sleep duration, sleep fragmentation, insomnia, circadian misalignment, and social jetlag.
Need more older-adult and adolescent studies measuring inflammatory markers and cognitive outcomes together.
Need wearable-based longitudinal models that distinguish subjective fatigue from objective cognitive impairment.
Need better recovery modeling after acute versus chronic sleep disruption.

most_supported_patterns: Insufficient or disrupted sleep reduces HRV and increases sympathetic/autonomic imbalance.
Circadian misalignment impairs glucose regulation and increases inflammatory/cardiovascular risk independent of total sleep duration.
Chronic sleep restriction produces cumulative attention/executive/working-memory impairment even when subjective fatigue plateaus.
highest_value_patterns_for_inference: Sleep disruption as an upstream hub node connecting wearable signals, metabolic state, inflammation, fatigue, mood, and cognition.
Reduced HRV and elevated resting heart rate as wearable-accessible mediators of poor recovery/autonomic imbalance.
Circadian misalignment as an independent risk node rather than merely a proxy for short sleep.
patterns_requiring_caution: Insomnia symptoms alone should not automatically imply reduced HRV.
Inflammatory mediation from sleep disturbance to fatigue/cognition is plausible but often not directly tested.
Subjective sleepiness/fatigue can underestimate objective cognitive impairment.
recommended_next_queries: Human studies linking HRV/resting heart rate/autonomic imbalance to fatigue, anxiety, attention, and cognitive performance.
Human studies on cortisol awakening response/evening cortisol, stress, fatigue, anxiety, and cognition.
Human studies on inflammation/cytokines and sickness behavior-like fatigue, mood, and cognitive impairment.
summary.overall_assessment: This sleep/circadian series is a strong hub for BodyState Mapper. Evidence is much stronger than the postprandial glucose-to-brain-fog pathway. The best graph additions are sleep disruption/circadian misalignment nodes linked to HRV, resting heart rate, insulin sensitivity, inflammation, fatigue, mood, and cognitive performance. Direct mediator chains should still be marked carefully unless within-person mediation is shown.
